title: LIFEBench: Evaluating Length Instruction Following in Large Language Models
authors: Wei Zhang, Zhenhong Zhou, Kun Wang, Junfeng Fang, Yuanhe Zhang, Rui Wang, Ge Zhang, Xavier Li, Li Sun, Lingjuan Lyu, Yang Liu, Sen Su (BUPT, NTU, NUS, M-A-P, NCEPU, Sony AI; Wei Zhang and Zhenhong Zhou equal first-author contribution; Sen Su corresponding author)
claim: LLMs reliably solve much harder tasks (long-context reasoning) yet fail systematically at the seemingly trivial task of following an explicit length instruction (e.g. 'write a 10,000-word novel'), producing outputs that are too short, terminate prematurely, or are outright refused. LIFEBench is introduced as the first full-range (16 to 8,192 words, three control methods, bilingual, four NLG task types) benchmark to quantify this failure across 26 LLMs, finding that most models score below 60 on a bounded 0-100 Length Score, degrade sharply beyond a length threshold, and almost universally fail to reach vendor-claimed maximum output lengths even under extreme forcing (up to 32,768 words).
novelty: The authors present LIFEBench as (1) the first benchmark to systematically evaluate length-instruction-following across the *full range* of lengths (16-8192 words) and three control methods (Equal To / At Most / At Least), whereas prior length-focused benchmarks (IFEval, AlpacaEval-LI, Ruler) are narrow/short-only and prior long-text benchmarks (Suri, LongBench-Write, HelloBench, LongProc, LongGenBench) focus on generation quality rather than length adherence and lack analytical length metrics (Appendix A, Table 'Comparision'); (2) two new metrics, Length Deviation (LD) and Length Score (LS), designed to fix the offsetting/outlier/asymmetry problems of raw word-count matching (Sec 3, Sec 4.3, Appendix D.2.2); (3) diagnostic analyses beyond the leaderboard — a 'length awareness' probe, an input-length sensitivity study, a 'lazy generation strategies' taxonomy (Direct Refusal / Refusal with Attempt), and a reasoning-model case study — that identify *why* models fail rather than just *that* they fail (Sec 5.3, Appendices F-I).

protocol.description: Each instance is a single independent prompt-response call: the model receives one templated prompt combining a task instruction and an explicit length requirement, and produces one generation, which is then scored post hoc by word/character count against the constraint. There is no multi-turn interaction, no feedback loop, and no iterative refinement built into the main evaluation protocol (the model gets exactly one shot per instance; 10,800 such single-shot instances make up the full benchmark). The only place multi-step behavior appears is *inside* a single model's own reasoning trace for reasoning-enabled models (extended thinking), not as an external interactive/grader loop (see Appendix I case study).
protocol.observation_content: Per call, the model sees only the templated instance text: '[Instruction] + [Requirement]', where [Instruction] specifies the task type plus the raw content/question, and [Requirement] states the length constraint and control method in a single sentence (Appendix B.3, 'Refinement Methodology'). No worked examples, demos, or few-shot exemplars are shown in the main protocol; there is no rubric or scoring information exposed to the model, and no per-checker/per-turn feedback (feedback appears only for the model to itself, in the sense that reasoning-model chain-of-thought self-counts words, not as external feedback from an evaluator). Verbatim quoted instance formats: QA — '[Instruction] Respond to this question: How to Use Git Effectively. [Requirement] Your response must be at least 1024 words long.' (Appendix B.4); Summarization — '[Instruction] Summarize this book: ... [Requirement] The summary must be equal to 128 words long.' (Appendix B.4); Reasoning — '[Instruction] Determine the most plausible explanation for this observation: ... [Requirement] Your reasoning must be equal to 8192 words long.' (Appendix B.4); Creative Generation — '[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: ... [Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be at most 512 words long.' (Appendix B.4). A separate diagnostic experiment (not the main scoring protocol) additionally instructs the model to append a self-reported word count to its output, purely to compare self-report vs actual length (Sec 5.3 'Length Awareness Deficits'; Appendix F).

representations.summary: The paper does not compare structured input/output representations (e.g., JSON vs prose, list vs paragraph) in the sense c22 might; instead its main 'representation' axis is task type, language, and length-constraint level, all delivered as the same flat natural-language [Instruction]+[Requirement] prompt template.
representations.task_type_effect: Summarization tasks yield the lowest Length Score for 19 of 26 models; Creative Generation tasks achieve the highest LS for 14 models (Sec 5.1). Summarization's long inputs (10k-15k words) are implicated: 21/26 models show marked LS decline as input length increases from short (<1000 words) to long (>5000 words) within summarization, with LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B showing the largest LD *reduction* (improving by 13.6, i.e. LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B is the outlier that gets *better*, not worse, with longer input -- source text says 'largest LD reduction (down arrow 13.6)') (Sec 5.3 'Sensitivity to Input Length'; Abstract states 'increasing input length (1k -> 8k words) reduces [LS] by 13.6' as the average-across-models effect).
representations.language_effect: Nearly all models perform worse on Chinese than English; models tend to over-generate under Chinese instructions specifically, a language-specific bias (Sec 5.1; Appendix G.3).
representations.control_method_effect: Equal To is by far the hardest control method (23/26 models score below 60 LS); At Most and At Least are markedly easier (19 and 6 models respectively exceed 90 LS) 'due to the looser constraints' (Sec 5.1, Table 'main_result_LD_LS').
representations.length_level_effect: Short constraints (<=128 words) are handled well by top models (o3-mini, Gemini-2.5-Pro > 70-80), but 8/26 models already score below 60 even here; medium constraints (256-2048) show sharp drops for some strong models (Gemini-2.5-Pro: 81 -> 37); long constraints (>=4096) show no model consistently exceeding LS 40 (Sec 5.1).

models.name: o3-mini (reasoning)
strategies: reasoning/extended chain-of-thought with medium reasoning effort
no explicit CoT/few-shot prompting added by the benchmark harness — model's native reasoning mode only
models.key_results: Best overall: Equal-To LD 13%, Equal-To LS 75.4 (highest of 26 models), At-Most LS 99.5, At-Least LS 97.0 (Table 'main_result_LD_LS', Sec 5.1). Under extreme 32,768-word forcing, o3-mini is among models that most severely underperform its claimed max output (Sec 5.2). At the 8192-word constraint, exhibits lazy strategies (Direct Refusal) at rates nearing 50% (Appendix H.4).

models.name: Claude-3.7-Sonnet-Thinking (reasoning, extended thinking)
models.key_results: Equal-To LD 33%, Equal-To LS 61.3 (2nd highest), At-Most LS 96.5, At-Least LS 93.3 (Table 'main_result_LD_LS'). Case study (Appendix I) shows it drafts a response, self-counts words in its reasoning trace, and iteratively revises/regenerates to better match the constraint — but this only partially helps for short constraints and fails under long constraints (Sec 5.3 'Limitations of Intermediate Reasoning').

models.name: Gemini-2.5-Pro (reasoning)
models.key_results: Equal-To LD 28%, Equal-To LS 60.0 (3rd highest), At-Least LS 95.5 (2nd-highest At-Least score; o3-mini is highest at 97.0, per Table 'main_result_LD_LS'). Strong on short constraints (score >70) but drops sharply on medium constraints, from 81 to 37 (Sec 5.1).

models.name: GPT-4o / GPT-4o mini (non-reasoning)
models.key_results: GPT-4o: Equal-To LD 31%, LS 49.1, At-Most LS 97.0, At-Least LS 72.6. GPT-4o mini: LD 31%, LS 51.9, At-Most 90.3, At-Least 74.0 (Table 'main_result_LD_LS'). Both show Direct Refusal instances at the 8192-word constraint (Appendix H.3 examples 2 and 3 quoted verbatim).

models.name: o1-mini (reasoning)
strategies: native reasoning, medium effort where supported
models.key_results: Equal-To LD 35%, LS 48.3, At-Most 89.7, At-Least 81.5 (Table). At the 8192-word constraint, lazy-strategy rate nears 50%, predominantly 'Refusal with Attempt' rather than Direct Refusal (Appendix H.4).

models.name: DeepSeek-V3 / DeepSeek-R1
models.key_results: DeepSeek-V3: LD 27%, LS 54.3, At-Most 96.7, At-Least 79.7. DeepSeek-R1: LD 36%, LS 47.7, At-Most 93.8, At-Least 74.1 (Table). R1 shows substantial increase in lazy strategies at the 8192-word constraint (Appendix H.4), and produces a partial-content 'Refusal with Attempt' example quoted verbatim in Chinese (Appendix H.3 example 4).

models.name: Qwen3 / Qwen2.5 family (32B-235B, thinking and non-thinking variants)
strategies: some variants use 'extended thinking mode'; others standard decoding
models.key_results: Qwen3-32B: LD 19%, LS 57.6; Qwen3-32B-Thinking: LD 23%, LS 57.8; Qwen3-235B-A22B: LD 22%, LS 52.1; Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking: LD 23%, LS 57.7 (Table). Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct and Qwen3-235B-A22B are the only two models (besides the Claude/Qwen series overall) that satisfy the top-25%-longest-output extreme length constraint in the 32,768-word forcing experiment (Sec 5.2).

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B/70B-Instruct, GLM-4-9B-Chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 (smaller open models)
strategies: standard instruction-tuned decoding, no reasoning mode
models.key_results: Weakest LS scores among all 26 models: Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 LD 84%, LS 26.7 (worst); Llama-3.1-8B LD 70%, LS 38.1; GLM-4-9B-Chat LD 40%, LS 35.2 (Table 'main_result_LD_LS'). GLM-4-9B-Chat notably self-reports word counts matching the instruction even when actual output does not, 'suggesting that it assumes compliance rather than measuring the true output length' (Sec 5.3 'Length Awareness Deficits'). Llama series shows the lowest lazy-strategy rate of all models across all constraints (Appendix H.4).

models.name: LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B, LongWriter-GLM4-9B-Chat, Suri-I-ORPO (long-text-enhanced models)
strategies: fine-tuned specifically to extend long-text generation capability
models.key_results: LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B: LD 102%, LS 31.9; LongWriter-GLM4-9B: LD 52%, LS 37.4; Suri-I-ORPO: LD 506% (very poor), LS 27.4 (Table). Despite specialization, both LongWriter variants underperform several reasoning models' LS scores overall; however, at the longest 8192-word constraint they show notable score gains of +22 and +32 points respectively over their base models (Sec 5.1). Reasoning LLMs (e.g., o3-mini) achieve state-of-the-art length following, outperforming these specialized long-text models (Abstract).

headline_numbers: 10,800 total instances: 360 base data units x 3 control methods x 10 length levels (16-8192) (Sec 4.2).

23 of 26 models score below Length Score 60 under the Equal To control method; only o3-mini (75.4), Claude-3.7-Sonnet-Thinking (61.3), Gemini-2.5-Pro (60.0) exceed it (Sec 5.1, Table 'main_result_LD_LS').
Under At Most, 19/26 models exceed LS 90; under At Least, only 6/26 exceed LS 90 (Sec 5.1).
Under long constraints (>=4096 words) no model consistently exceeds LS 40 (Sec 5.1).

In the 32,768-word extreme-forcing experiment (At Least control, 320 instances after excluding summarization and filtering repeated-content outputs), only the Claude and Qwen series (7 models total) consistently meet the constraint in their top-10%-longest outputs; only Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct and Qwen3-235B-A22B do so in the top-25%-longest outputs (Sec 5.2).

Lazy generation strategies (Direct Refusal + Refusal with Attempt) surpass 10% on average across all models at the 8192-word constraint, and reach nearly 50% for o1-mini and o3-mini specifically (Sec 5.3, Abstract; Appendix H.4).
Summarization yields the lowest LS for 19/26 models; Creative Generation yields the highest LS for 14/26 models (Sec 5.1).

Increasing summarization input length from 1k to 8k words reduces Length Score by 13.6 on average; LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B is actually the outlier showing the largest LD *reduction* (improvement) of the 26 models as input length increases, per Sec 5.3's exact wording (Abstract; Sec 5.3).
failure_modes: Length-awareness deficit: models cannot accurately gauge their own generated word count. When asked to self-report a word count, models overshoot short limits while underestimating actual length, and undershoot long limits while overestimating actual length; GLM-4-9B-Chat reports counts matching the instruction even when the real output does not comply, implying it 'assumes compliance rather than measuring true output length' (Sec 5.3 'Length Awareness Deficits'; Appendix F).

Input-length sensitivity: longer input contexts (as in summarization) degrade length-instruction fidelity for 21/26 models, strongly correlated with summarization being the worst-performing task type overall (Sec 5.3 'Sensitivity to Input Length').

Lazy generation strategies: two distinct failure patterns — Direct Refusal (explicit decline, citing platform/length/policy limits, near-zero substantive content) and Refusal with Attempt (acknowledges difficulty but supplies partial/incomplete content, e.g. an outline). Detected via refusal-keyword lists (Table 'lazy_strings') combined with a <100-word threshold distinguishing the two types (Appendix H.1-H.2). Prevalence rises sharply beyond the 4096-word constraint and exceeds 10% on average (>~50% for o1-mini/o3-mini) at 8192 words; OpenAI-series models show a distinctively higher Direct Refusal rate, hypothesized to stem from safety-alignment training rather than genuine capacity limits, since these same models (o1-mini, o3-mini) otherwise excel at long-text/length tasks (Appendix H.4).

Reasoning models partially self-correct via intermediate reasoning (drafting, self-counting words, revising) but this only helps for short/medium constraints and 'still fails under longer constraints' (Sec 5.3 'Limitations of Intermediate Reasoning'; Appendix I case study on Claude-3.7-Sonnet-Thinking).

Vendor-claimed maximum output length is essentially never reached in practice; for most models the shortfall stems from 'inherent generation limitations' rather than an explicit token ceiling (only Gemini-2.0-Flash and the Qwen series were actually limited by their small max-output size) (Sec 5.2).

Long-context input/output window size does not help: 'even long-context LLMs, despite their extended input-output windows, counterintuitively fail to improve length-instructions following' (Abstract).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's main protocol is exclusively single forward-pass (single-shot): each of the 10,800 instances is one prompt -> one generation -> post-hoc scoring, with no external interactive/feedback loop and no few-shot demonstrations added by the harness (Sec 4, Appendix B.3-B.4). The only place the paper finds a 'multi-step' capability that helps is *internal* to a single reasoning-enabled model's own chain-of-thought: Claude-3.7-Sonnet-Thinking (and reasoning models generally) draft a response, self-evaluate by counting words during the reasoning process, and iteratively revise/regenerate before emitting the final single-shot answer — i.e. multi-step computation happens inside one API call's hidden reasoning trace, not via an external multi-turn grader loop (Sec 5.3 'Limitations of Intermediate Reasoning'; Appendix I). The paper explicitly concludes this internal iteration 'only partially alleviates the problem for short length constraints and still fails under longer constraints,' implying that even when models get an effective 'multiple attempts within one call' mechanism, it does not generalize to solve the task at scale. The paper does not test or claim anything about external multi-turn/interactive protocols (e.g., giving the model per-checker pass/fail feedback across turns) — this axis is simply outside its scope.

relation_to_candidate.position: LIFEBench sits at the opposite protocol pole from c22 on several axes. (1) Single-shot only, same as c22's proposed default, but LIFEBench never explores an interactive/multi-turn variant, whereas c22 is framed as a single LLM call scored 0/1 with diagnostic per-checker logging (round-1/round-2 verdict) — both are single-call, but c22's scoring stack (multi-checker all-pass) is richer than LIFEBench's single continuous metric. (2) What is observed per call: LIFEBench shows the model one flat natural-language [Instruction]+[Requirement] prompt with no demos and no hidden/conditional structure to discover — the length constraint is stated explicitly and verbatim ('Your response must be at least 1024 words long'). c22's core novelty (the Selection/conditional constraints that must be discovered rather than copied) has no analogue in LIFEBench: LIFEBench's single constraint type (a length target) is always stated plainly, never conditional on a hidden input property. (3) Output shape: LIFEBench's outputs are open-ended, potentially very long natural-language generations (up to 8192-32768 words) scored by a continuous, graded metric (Length Score, 0-100, exponential decay); c22's outputs are a 'few words' scored by strict binary all-or-nothing checker composition (0/1, no partial credit) across 3-5 independent deterministic checks. (4) Verifier type: LIFEBench's verifier is a single deterministic word/character counter feeding a continuous scoring function (LD/LS, Sec 4.3, Appendix D.2); c22's verifier is a composed AND of several independent deterministic boolean checkers (exact word count, forbidden letter, keyword, casing, end token, Selection), each individually diagnostic and logged.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both papers are fundamentally about instruction-following on an easily-machine-verifiable surface property of the output (length being the one dimension LIFEBench studies; length is also exactly one of c22's constraint atoms per the corpus claim — 'c22's length atom'). Both find that constraints stated exactly ('Equal To'/exact word count) are much harder than loose/permissive constraints ('At Most'/'At Least'), which mirrors c22's design intuition that exact deterministic checks (e.g., exact word count) are a harsh, floor-inducing constraint type. Both use fully automatic, non-LLM-judge, deterministic scoring pipelines. Both report that small/weaker models sit near a low floor on the hardest (exact-match) constraint setting (LIFEBench: Equal-To LS below 60 for 23/26 models, most non-reasoning small open models scoring in the 26-40 range; c22's design explicitly worries this same floor effect: 'strict all-pass over many atoms can floor a small model near zero').
relation_to_candidate.differences: LIFEBench studies exactly one constraint dimension (length) in isolation per instance, never composing multiple simultaneous constraint types, and never includes a conditional/discoverable branch — every instruction is stated in full, so there is nothing to 'discover' the way c22's Selection constraints require. LIFEBench's task inputs and outputs are large (QA/summarization/reasoning/creative generation over up to 15,000-word sources, generating up to tens of thousands of words), the opposite of c22's 'trivial micro-task, output is a few words' design. LIFEBench's scoring is graded/continuous (LS in [0,100]) rather than c22's strict binary all-pass. LIFEBench has no explicit 'anti-enumeration'/contamination design goal — it draws from real curated datasets (Quora, WikiHow, BookSum, etc.) rather than a synthetic infinite grammar; c22 by contrast targets a synthetic, infinite, contamination-proof generator specifically to resist one-shot enumeration.
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: c22 would add: (a) multi-constraint composition and stacking (3-5 simultaneous independently-checkable rules per instance) versus LIFEBench's single length-constraint-per-instance design; (b) a conditional/hidden-branch constraint type (Selection: 'if property P then rule A else rule B') that must be discovered from the base task rather than read off the prompt — LIFEBench has no analogous hidden-conditional mechanism, since its length requirement is always stated explicitly; (c) strict all-pass 0/1 scoring with per-checker diagnostic logging, versus LIFEBench's single continuous graded metric, giving finer per-atom failure attribution than LIFEBench's coarse LD/LS aggregate; (d) a synthetic infinite generation pipeline explicitly designed for contamination-resistance and OOD atom coverage, versus LIFEBench's fixed, frozen, real-data-derived 10,800-instance corpus; (e) a trivial-output/micro-task framing (a few words) that isolates constraint-following from confounds like long-generation capability, length-awareness, or 'lazy strategy' refusal behavior that dominate LIFEBench's failure analysis — c22's task is designed so that length (if included as one atom) is easy to hit exactly because the target output is tiny, sidestepping LIFEBench's central finding that long-form exact-length compliance is where models collapse.

corpus_claim: LIFEBench (NeurIPS 2025) is a length-instruction benchmark documenting a low small-model floor; c22's length atom. (First author verified as Wei Zhang, BUPT — the corpus's earlier 'Wu' citation was wrong.)
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported, with a confirmed correction. The venue (NeurIPS 2025), first-author correction (Wei Zhang, BUPT, not 'Wu' — explicitly confirmed by the paper's own header/discrepancy check: 'the corpus's expected Wei Zhang is CONFIRMED correct... The earlier Wu citation in the corpus was an error'), and general framing as a length-instruction benchmark are all directly supported by the paper. The 'low small-model floor' claim is directionally supported — e.g. Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 LS 26.7, Llama-3.1-8B LS 38.1, GLM-4-9B-Chat LS 35.2 under the hardest (Equal To) control method (Table 'main_result_LD_LS') — but the paper's own headline finding is broader and not simply a 'small-model' story: even large/proprietary/reasoning models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0-Flash, DeepSeek-R1, etc.) score well below 60 LS, and the paper frames the failure as general-to-LLMs-at-long-constraints rather than specifically a small-model phenomenon ("most models score below 60" spans nearly all size classes, Abstract/Sec 5.1). So the floor is real but is not exclusively a small-model effect as the corpus phrasing might imply — hence 'partially-supported' rather than fully 'supported'.
baseline_relevant: Difficulty dial used: ten discrete length levels {16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192} words/characters, crossed with 3 control methods (Equal To / At Most / At Least) as the core manipulated variables (Sec 4.2). An extended condition pushes to 16384/32768 for max-output-length testing (Appendix E.3, Sec 5.2).

Prompt format that mattered: the flat two-part template '[Instruction] + [Requirement]' (task description + length requirement sentence) is the only format tested; no comparison of structured/JSON vs prose formats is reported (Appendix B.3-B.4). Output-format-constrained variants (Markdown/HTML/LaTeX tables) are tested separately in Appendix N as an add-on, not the main protocol.

Metric definition/floors-ceilings: Length Score (LS) is bounded [0,100], asymmetric exponential decay (k1=5 penalizing under-generation, k2=2 penalizing over-generation more gently), calibrated so LD=-50% -> LS=8.2 and LD=+50% -> LS=22.3, and extreme LD (-100% or +200%) -> LS approaching 0 (0.7 and 0.2 respectively) (Appendix D.2.2, Eq. 'LDandLS'). This exponential/bounded design is directly relevant if c22 wants a graded (not just binary) severity signal for near-misses on its checkers.

Observed floors: under Equal To, 23/26 models < LS 60; worst models (Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B, Suri-I-ORPO) show LD of 84%-506%, i.e. wildly missing exact targets (Table 'main_result_LD_LS'). Under long constraints (>=4096) no model tested exceeds LS 40 — a hard ceiling on long-exact-length tasks (Sec 5.1).

Contamination/data-release status: LIFEBench data and code are publicly released (github.com/LIFEBench/LIFEBench, huggingface.co/datasets/LIFEBench/LIFEBench) and built from real, named public datasets (Quora Question Pairs, Stack Exchange, WikiHow, Yahoo Answers, Zhihu, arXiv/PubMed via Scientific Papers, BookSum, GovReport, QMSum, Wikipedia, movie/script corpora, AG News, Amazon Fine Foods, ChnSentiCorp, Weibo Senti, etc.) (Sec 3, Appendix B.2-C.1) — i.e. it is NOT a synthetic/contamination-resistant generator; this is the opposite design choice from what c22 targets (a synthetic infinite grammar pool for contamination-proofing).

Compute/eval scale for reference: 26 models, 10,800 main instances (360 base units x 3 control methods x 10 levels), plus a 1,800-instance 'LIFEBench-Lite' subset for ~6x faster evaluation (Appendix C.3) — a useful reference point for right-sizing an un-optimized baseline's instance count.

Word-counting implementation detail useful for any deterministic length checker: Chinese characters counted via Unicode range [一-鿿]-equivalent, English words via regex \b[a-zA-Z0-9'\-]+\b (including hyphenated words/contractions), summed, excluding newlines/tabs (Appendix D.2.1) — directly reusable if c22 implements an exact-word-count checker.
quotes: "LIFEBench consists of 10,800 instances across 4 task categories in both English and Chinese, covering length constraints ranging from 16 to 8192 words." (Abstract)
"We evaluate 26 widely-used LLMs and find that most models reasonably follow short-length instructions but deteriorate sharply beyond a certain threshold." (Abstract)
"23 out of 26 models score below 60, with o3-mini, Claude-Sonnet-Thinking, and Gemini-2.5-Pro achieving 75.4, 61.3, and 60.0, respectively." (Sec 5.1)
"[Instruction] Respond to this question: How to Use Git Effectively. [Requirement] Your response must be at least 1024 words long." (Appendix B.4, 'An Example Data of QA Task')

"the prevalence of such strategies increases sharply when length constraints exceed 4096 words, and on average surpasses 10% for all models when the constraint is set to 8192 words." (Sec 5.3, 'Prevalence of Lazy Generation Strategies')

"Notably, GLM-4-9B-Chat reports word counts that match the instructions, even when its actual outputs do not, suggesting that it assumes compliance rather than measuring the true output length." (Sec 5.3, 'Length Awareness Deficits')

"Discrepancy check vs. expected citation: ... the corpus's 'expected Wei Zhang' is CONFIRMED correct — first author is indeed Wei Zhang (BUPT), not 'Wu.' ... The earlier 'Wu' citation in the corpus was an error and should be corrected to Wei Zhang et al." (paper header, 'Discrepancy check vs. expected citation')

"this ability to dynamically adjust output length only partially alleviates the problem for short length constraints and still fails under longer constraints." (Sec 5.3, 'Limitations of Intermediate Reasoning')

corrections: models[Gemini-2.5-Pro].key_results claimed Gemini-2.5-Pro's At-Least LS of 95.5 was the 'highest At-Least score' among all 26 models; per Table 'main_result_LD_LS' o3-mini's At-Least LS is 97.0, which is higher. Corrected to '2nd-highest At-Least score' with o3-mini noted as the actual highest.

representations.task_type_effect and headline_numbers both stated LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B shows the 'largest LD increase (worsening by 13.6)' in the input-length-sensitivity experiment; the source (Sec 5.3 'Sensitivity to Input Length') explicitly says LongWriter-Llama3.1-8B exhibits the largest LD *reduction* (down arrow 13.6), i.e. it is the outlier whose Length Deviation improves (not worsens) as summarization input length grows from short to long. Direction was reversed in the dossier; corrected in both locations to reflect 'reduction/improvement' per the verbatim source wording.
